title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 13 (Fragment 6)
text:
Behind them was a perfect clamor of Petrocchi voices, all wanting Renata. “He just does,” Paolo said. “Be quick!”

The small door to the street had a big stiff key. Renata took it in both hands and struggled to turn it. “Does he know you ?” she said sharply.

Like an answer, Marco’s voice sounded from behind the coach. “Renata!” Then, much more softly, “Paolo—Paolo Montana, come here!”

The door came open. “Run, if you’re coming!” Paolo said. They shot out into the street, both running hard. Marco came to the door and shouted something, but he did not seem to be following. Nevertheless, Paolo kept on running, which forced Renata to run too. He did not want to talk. He wanted to absorb the shock of Marco. Marco Andretti was really Marco Petrocchi—he must be Guido’s eldest son! Rosa Montana and Marco Petrocchi. How did they do it? How ever did they manage it? he kept wondering. And also—more soberly—How ever will they get away with it?

“All right. That will do,” Renata panted. By this time they had crossed the Corso and were down beside the river, trotting along empty quaysides towards the New Bridge. Renata slowed down, and Paolo did too, quite breathless. “Now,” she said, “tell me how Marco knew you, or I won’t come a step farther.”

Paolo looked at her warily. He had already discovered that Renata was, as Aunt Gina would say, sharp enough to cut herself, and he did not like the way she was looking at him. “He saw me at the Palace of course,” Paolo said.

“No he didn’t,” said Renata. “He drove the coach. He knows your name and he knows why you came, doesn’t he? How?”

“I think he must have been standing behind us on the Art Gallery steps, and we didn’t see him in the fog,” said Paolo.

Renata’s shrewd eyes continued the look Paolo did not like. “Good try,” she said. Paolo tried to break off the look by turning and sauntering on along the quays. Renata followed him, saying, “And I was meant to get all embarrassed and not ask any more. You’re sharp enough to cut yourself, Mr. Montana. But what a pity. Marco wasn’t in the fight. They wanted him for the single combat, that’s how I know, and he wasn’t there, so Papa had to do it. And I can tell that you don’t want me to know how Marco knows you. And I can tell Marco doesn’t, or he’d have stopped me going by saying who you were. So—”

“You’re the one who’s going to cut yourself,” Paolo said over his shoulder, “by being too clever. I don’t know how Marco knew me, but he was being kind not say—” He stopped. He sniffed. He was level with an alleyway, where a peeling blue house bulged out onto the jetty. Paolo felt the air around that alley with a sense he hardly knew he had, inborn over generations of spell-making. A spell had been set here—a strong spell, not long ago.

Renata came up behind. “You’re not going to wriggle out—” She stopped too. “Someone made a spell here!”

“Was it Angelica? Can you tell?” Paolo asked.

“Why?” said Renata.

Paolo told her what Chrestomanci had said. Her face went red, and she prodded with her toe at a mooring chain in the path. “Individual style!” she said. “Him and his jokes! It’s not Angelica’s fault. She was born that way. And it’s not everyone who can get a spell to work by doing everything wrong. I think she’s a sort of back-to-front genius, and I told the Duchess of Caprona so when she laughed, too!”

vocabulary.definition: To take in or soak up (energy, information, or a liquid).
vocabulary.example: He wanted to absorb the shock of Marco.

vocabulary.definition: In reversed order or position.
vocabulary.example: I think she’s a sort of back-to-front genius, and I told the Duchess of Caprona so when she laughed, too!

vocabulary.definition: Unable to breathe easily or needing to catch one's breath.
vocabulary.example: Renata slowed down, and Paolo did too, quite breathless.

vocabulary.definition: To swell or protrude outward.
vocabulary.example: He was level with an alleyway, where a peeling blue house bulged out onto the jetty.

vocabulary.definition: A loud and confused noise, especially that of people shouting.
vocabulary.example: Behind them was a perfect clamor of Petrocchi voices, all wanting Renata.

vocabulary.definition: A horse-drawn carriage or a bus used for longer journeys.
vocabulary.example: Like an answer, Marco’s voice sounded from behind the coach.

vocabulary.definition: A fight or battle.
vocabulary.example: They wanted him for the single combat, that’s how I know, and he wasn’t there, so Papa had to do it.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling self-conscious or ashamed.
vocabulary.example: Renata followed him, saying, “And I was meant to get all embarrassed and not ask any more.

vocabulary.definition: Exceptional intellectual or creative power.
vocabulary.example: I think she’s a sort of back-to-front genius, and I told the Duchess of Caprona so when she laughed, too!

vocabulary.definition: Existing from birth; innate.
vocabulary.example: Paolo felt the air around that alley with a sense he hardly knew he had, inborn over generations of spell-making.

vocabulary.definition: A distinctive or unique way of expressing oneself.
vocabulary.example: “Individual style!

vocabulary.definition: To continue doing something.
vocabulary.example: Nevertheless, Paolo kept on running, which forced Renata to run too.

vocabulary.definition: A place or device for securing a boat or ship to shore.
vocabulary.example: Her face went red, and she prodded with her toe at a mooring chain in the path.

vocabulary.definition: To breathe quickly and loudly, usually after exertion.
vocabulary.example: That will do,” Renata panted.

vocabulary.definition: Coming off or flaking away in small pieces.
vocabulary.example: He was level with an alleyway, where a peeling blue house bulged out onto the jetty.

vocabulary.definition: The area beside a quay where ships dock and load or unload.
vocabulary.example: By this time they had crossed the Corso and were down beside the river, trotting along empty quaysides towards the New Bridge.

vocabulary.definition: To walk in a slow, relaxed manner.
vocabulary.example: Paolo tried to break off the look by turning and sauntering on along the quays.

vocabulary.definition: In a severe or abrupt manner.
vocabulary.example: ” she said sharply.

vocabulary.definition: Having or showing sharp judgment; astute.
vocabulary.example: Renata’s shrewd eyes continued the look Paolo did not like.

vocabulary.definition: In a serious, sensible, and solemn manner.
vocabulary.example: And also—more soberly—How ever will they get away with it?

vocabulary.definition: Words or actions supposed to have magical power.
vocabulary.example: A spell had been set here—a strong spell, not long ago.

vocabulary.definition: The process or act of creating magical spells.
vocabulary.example: Paolo felt the air around that alley with a sense he hardly knew he had, inborn over generations of spell-making.

vocabulary.definition: Not easily bent or changed in shape; rigid.
vocabulary.example:
”

The small door to the street had a big stiff key.

vocabulary.definition: In a cautious or suspicious manner.
vocabulary.example:
”

Paolo looked at her warily.
